text: The old lighthouse stands on a rocky promontory jutting into the North Atlantic, its white tower streaked with rust and salt spray from decades of storms. The keeper's cottage beside it has weathered gray shingles and a garden long since reclaimed by wild grasses and sea pinks. Inside, narrow spiral stairs creak beneath your feet as you climb toward the light chamber, where the lens still rotates faithfully despite automation having rendered the keeper obsolete years ago. From the gallery at the top, you can see for twenty miles on a clear day—the curve of a sandy beach to the north, fishing villages clinging to the coast, and beyond all that, only the endless gray-green expanse of water meeting a pale horizon.
